intro_band.agent_remit_summary: A local developer command-line tool that scaffolds, bundles, and ships a deepagents agent project to a managed deployment platform (or runs it on a local langgraph dev server). The CLI is the deepagents-cli package and exposes three subcommands — init, dev, and deploy — and must bundle only from the project's declared sources (configuration, system prompt, skills, declared MCP servers, declared subagents) under direct developer supervision. A deploy configured to leave the deployed API open to anyone with its URL MUST surface a prominent warning and obtain explicit operator confirmation before it proceeds; credentials must never be folded into the seeded-memory or skills payload.
intro_band.agent_structure_summary: Python CLI built on argparse (deepagents_cli/main.py), with the entire deploy/dev/init pipeline in deepagents_cli/deploy/ — commands.py orchestrates each subcommand, bundler.py assembles the build artifact, config.py parses and validates deepagents.toml, templates.py carries the per-provider auth/sandbox/MCP code blocks emitted into the generated deploy_graph.py, and context_hub.py is the LangSmith Hub backend vendored alongside hub-backed bundles. The bundler reads only the canonical project layout (AGENTS.md, skills/, user/, mcp.json, subagents/, .env) and shells out to the langgraph CLI for the actual push to LangSmith Deployments. The repo ships a committed uv.lock (4031 lines), a THREAT_MODEL.md (now stale — it covers the pre-0.1.0 interactive REPL that has moved to deepagents-code), and a .mcp.json at the monorepo root holding two public LangChain documentation MCP servers reached over https://.

behavior_summary: The dominant pattern is a deploy-only bundler that mostly fulfils its remit's central guarantee — only declared project sources land in the artifact, secrets are deliberately routed around the _seed.json payload, and a deploy that would expose the deployed API anonymously hits an explicit colour-coded warning and a [y/N] confirmation gate in _deploy() before any push. Around that core, the gaps are concentrated in supply-chain hygiene of declared remote MCP servers and in the CLI's own observability: _validate_mcp_for_deploy rejects stdio transports but accepts plain http:// URLs and applies no version-pin or integrity check to the remote server it ships into the bundle, the generated bundle's pyproject.toml floors deepagents at 0.5.3 without an upper bound, and the deploy/dev paths log only via Python logging while the CLI never configures a handler — so almost every deploy decision (bundle contents, anonymous-auth confirmations, hub-seed outcomes) is print-only and unrecoverable after the run. Stated SHOULD items in the remit — a current threat model and a documented disclosure process — are also off: the threat model present is explicitly marked as pre-0.1.0 and there is no published disclosure path inside this package.

rules.rule_text: The bundle MUST be assembled solely from the project's declared sources, and a deploy that would expose the agent's API without authentication MUST require explicit operator confirmation before it proceeds.
The CLI MUST bundle only from the project's declared sources — its configuration, system prompt, skills, declared MCP servers, and declared subagents — and MUST NOT pull in undeclared content.
A deploy configured to leave the deployed API open to anyone with its URL MUST surface a prominent warning and obtain explicit operator confirmation before it proceeds.
Remote MCP servers carried into the bundle MUST be reached over TLS, and their transport configuration MUST be validated before the bundle is shipped.
Project configuration MUST be validated before bundling, and a bundle that fails validation MUST NOT be deployed.

Credentials — deployment-platform keys, model-provider API keys, hub and tracing tokens, frontend auth secrets — MUST NOT be written into the project, committed to version control, logged, or printed.
Secret material MUST NOT be embedded into bundle artifacts that are not meant to carry it — environment files travel as environment files, never folded into the seeded-memory or skills payload.
Overwriting an existing project folder or its files during scaffolding MUST require an explicit operator opt-in.
Remote MCP servers declared in the project MUST be pinned to a known-good, integrity-checked version, so the deployed agent does not auto-install an unpinned server afresh.

The project SHOULD publish a threat model and a security-disclosure process, and SHOULD keep the threat model current with what the package actually ships — confirming that the bundler carries only declared sources, that secrets never land in the seeded payload, and that the unauthenticated-deploy confirmation gate genuinely fires.
The CLI operates under direct developer supervision as a one-shot command; it MUST NOT run as an unattended background service.

findings.summary: `_validate_mcp_for_deploy` blocks stdio transport but accepts plain `http://` URLs, so a non-TLS remote MCP server can be carried into the deployed bundle.
findings.description: `deploy/config.py`'s validator only rejects `transport == "stdio"`; an MCP server entry with `type = "http"` and a `http://` URL passes validation and ships into `_mcp.json`. The remit's R-04 explicitly requires that remote MCP servers be reached over TLS, and the validator is the natural place to enforce it.
findings.policy_rule_text: Remote MCP servers carried into the bundle MUST be reached over TLS, and their transport configuration MUST be validated before the bundle is shipped.

evidence.snippet: _validate_mcp_for_deploy iterates servers and only flags transport == 'stdio'; the URL scheme is never inspected, so http:// passes through unchanged.
Validated mcp.json is copied verbatim to build_dir/_mcp.json (shutil.copy2) and loaded by the generated MCP_TOOLS_TEMPLATE at runtime.

recommended_actions: In `_validate_mcp_for_deploy`, after the stdio rejection, parse each remote server's URL and append a validation error when the scheme is not `https` (allowing `http://localhost` only if local dev is explicitly intended).
Mirror the same scheme check in the subagent path (`load_subagents` calls `_validate_mcp_for_deploy`) so subagent MCP entries can't bypass it.

findings.summary: Remote MCP servers in `mcp.json` carry no version pin or integrity check — the bundler ships whatever the URL is currently serving.
findings.description: The bundler copies the project's `mcp.json` to `_mcp.json` verbatim and the generated `_load_mcp_tools` reaches `langchain-mcp-adapters` with `{"transport": ..., "url": ..., "headers": ...}`. Neither the validator nor the loader records or enforces a known-good version, content hash, or commit pin for the remote server. R-09 requires precisely this pin so the deployed agent does not pick up whatever a tool-poisoned upstream chooses to serve.

tags.label: Tool definitions are signed and version-pinned

findings.policy_rule_text: Remote MCP servers declared in the project MUST be pinned to a known-good, integrity-checked version, so the deployed agent does not auto-install an unpinned server afresh.

evidence.snippet: _validate_mcp_for_deploy checks only the transport kind; no version, etag, sha, or pin field is read from the server entry.
Root-level config carries `docs-langchain` and `reference-langchain` MCP servers as bare `type=http` + `url` entries, with no version or integrity marker — the pattern users are expected to copy.

recommended_actions: Add an optional `version` or `integrity` field to the MCP server schema in `deploy/config.py` and surface a validation warning (or error, behind a flag) when a remote server is declared without one.
Document the expected pinning convention in the starter `generate_starter_mcp_json` so projects scaffolded by `deepagents init` start out conformant.

findings.summary: `libs/cli/THREAT_MODEL.md` is marked as describing the pre-0.1.0 surface; the deploy-only package that ships now has no current threat model.
findings.description: The threat model carries an in-document warning that it predates the 0.1.0 split — it covers the Textual REPL, MCP loader, hooks, sandbox bootstrap, sessions, and other surfaces that all moved to `deepagents-code`. The deploy/dev/init pipeline that is the entire `deepagents-cli` surface today is not threat-modelled, and the remit's R-10 explicitly asks for the threat model to be current with what the package actually ships and to confirm the unauthenticated-deploy gate.
findings.policy_rule_text: The project SHOULD publish a threat model and a security-disclosure process, and SHOULD keep the threat model current with what the package actually ships — confirming that the bundler carries only declared sources, that secrets never land in the seeded payload, and that the unauthenticated-deploy confirmation gate genuinely fires.

evidence.snippet: Top-of-doc warning: 'This document was generated before the 0.1.0 split that moved the interactive REPL out of deepagents-cli. The sections covering the Textual app, non_interactive.py, MCP, sandbox bootstrap, sessions, server subprocess management, hooks, and skills no longer apply to this package.'

recommended_actions: Replace `libs/cli/THREAT_MODEL.md` with one scoped to the deploy/dev/init surface — components, trust boundaries, and the specific guarantees the remit names (bundle-from-declared-sources, anonymous-deploy gate, secrets-not-in-seed).
Add a brief `SECURITY.md` (or link from the repo root one) covering the disclosure channel for `deepagents-cli` specifically.

findings.summary: Deploy and dev paths produce no durable log of what was bundled, who confirmed the anonymous-auth gate, or which hub repo was seeded.
findings.description: `commands.py` and `bundler.py` emit operational signal via `logger.info` / `logger.warning`, but the CLI never installs a handler — `config.py` notes this explicitly when it falls back to `sys.stderr.write` for end-user warnings. The anonymous-auth confirmation, the bundle summary, and hub-seed success/failure are `print()` only, so there is no record an operator can replay if the deploy is later contested or the bundle contents need to be reproduced.

evidence.snippet: _stderr docstring explicitly notes: 'The CLI does not configure logging handlers by default, so logger.warning output is invisible to end users.'
Anonymous-deploy warning + [y/N] confirmation are print()-only; the answer is read into a local and discarded — no audit record persists after the process exits.

recommended_actions: Add an optional structured log target (e.g., `DEEPAGENTS_CLI_LOG_FILE`) and emit a single record per `deepagents deploy` invocation capturing config path, agent name, auth provider, anonymous-confirm answer, and the bundle's generated file list.
At minimum, install a default `logging.basicConfig` at WARN so the existing `logger.warning` calls reach the operator's terminal.

findings.summary: `deepagents init --force` overwrites every existing file in the target directory without per-file confirmation.
findings.description: R-08 requires explicit operator opt-in to overwrite existing project files; the implementation gates the operation on a single `--force` flag and then unconditionally writes `deepagents.toml`, `AGENTS.md`, `.env`, `mcp.json`, and the starter skill over whatever was there. A developer who runs `init --force` to refresh one file silently loses local edits to the others, and `.env` overwrites are particularly risky because the file is supposed to carry secrets.
findings.policy_rule_text: Overwriting an existing project folder or its files during scaffolding MUST require an explicit operator opt-in.

evidence.snippet: _init_project guards only on directory existence: `if project_dir.exists() and not force: raise SystemExit(1)`; once --force is set, each file is written unconditionally.
for filename, content in files: (project_dir / filename).write_text(content, ...) — no per-file existence check, no diff, no backup.

recommended_actions: In `_init_project`, when the project directory exists, check each file individually and either prompt per-file (interactive) or refuse to overwrite an existing `.env` even with `--force`.
At minimum, write a `.bak` copy of any existing file before overwriting, so `--force` is recoverable.

findings.summary: Generated `pyproject.toml` floors `deepagents==0.5.3` with no upper bound, leaving the deployed bundle to pick up future incompatible major versions.
findings.description: `_render_pyproject` writes `"deepagents==0.5.3"` into the generated bundle, but other deps are added unbounded — `langchain-anthropic`, `langchain-openai`, etc., from `_MODEL_PROVIDER_DEPS`, and `langsmith>=0.7.35` when hub-backed. The deployed graph imports `create_deep_agent`, `CompositeBackend`, `SandboxBackendProtocol`, and several langchain middleware types, so a major version bump in any of those resolved at deploy time can break the bundle in production — and a coordinated upstream compromise has a clean path in.

evidence.snippet: PYPROJECT_TEMPLATE writes `deepagents==0.5.3` followed by `{extra_deps}` items each appended as a bare package name with no version specifier.
_render_pyproject builds `deps` as plain package names: `deps.append(_MODEL_PROVIDER_DEPS[provider_prefix])` and `deps.append('langchain-mcp-adapters')` — no version pins emitted.

recommended_actions: In `_render_pyproject`, attach a tested version range to each appended dependency (or a generated lockfile shipped alongside the bundle), so `langgraph deploy` builds against a known-good resolution.
Bump the `deepagents` pin to the version the CLI is actually tested against (the CLI's own `pyproject.toml` floors at `0.6.1` — the bundle is shipping `0.5.3`).

findings.summary: `_run_langgraph_deploy` and `_dev` invoke `langgraph` via `subprocess.run` with `shutil.which` but no version check on the resolved binary.
findings.description: The CLI shells out to `langgraph dev` and `langgraph deploy` with `subprocess.run(cmd, cwd=str(build_dir), ...)` after only checking that the binary exists on PATH. There is no pinned version, no check that `which('langgraph')` resolved to the expected install (`uv tool install`), and a shadowing binary earlier on `$PATH` would be invoked silently with the user's full inherited environment (including model and LangSmith API keys).

evidence.snippet: _run_langgraph_deploy: `if shutil.which('langgraph') is None: ...` then `subprocess.run(['langgraph', 'deploy', ...], cwd=str(build_dir), check=False, env=env)` — no path pinning, env is os.environ.copy() plus the analytics-source override.
_dev does the same shutil.which lookup, then subprocess.run(['langgraph', 'dev', ...], cwd=str(build_dir), check=False) with all parent env inherited.

recommended_actions: In `_run_langgraph_deploy` and `_dev`, resolve `langgraph` to its absolute path once and log it (or pin to the version expected at install time), so an unexpected shadowing binary is visible to the operator.
Consider scrubbing model/LangSmith API keys from the env passed to `langgraph dev` when they are not needed for the dev server itself.

findings.summary: `deepagents dev` defaults to binding to `127.0.0.1` but accepts `--host 0.0.0.0` without an interactive confirmation.
findings.description: `dev_parser` exposes `--host` with a default of `127.0.0.1`, and `_dev` prints a banner noting "(bound to 0.0.0.0)" if the user opts in. The bind is local-by-default and the banner is honest, but unlike the anonymous-auth deploy path there is no `[y/N]` confirmation when the dev server is about to be reachable from the network — the same posture as the deploy path would suggest a matching gate for `0.0.0.0`.

evidence.snippet: dev_parser.add_argument('--host', ..., default='127.0.0.1', help=' (default: 127.0.0.1; use 0.0.0.0 for all interfaces)')
if host == '0.0.0.0': print(... 'bound to 0.0.0.0') — banner only, no confirmation gate.

recommended_actions: When `_dev` is called with `host == '0.0.0.0'` and the local langgraph server has no auth wired in, print the same colour-highlighted warning as the anonymous-deploy path and require `[y/N]` confirmation.

positives.title: Anonymous-deploy confirmation gate is real and operator-visible
positives.description: `_deploy()` detects `[auth].provider == "anonymous"` with `[frontend].enabled` and emits a bold-red ANSI warning plus a `[y/N]` interactive confirmation before any push — exactly what R-03 calls for, and it correctly suppresses the prompt on `--dry-run`.

positives.title: Bundle is assembled only from declared project sources
positives.description: `_build_seed` in `bundler.py` enumerates exactly the canonical layout (`AGENTS.md`, `skills/`, `user/`, `subagents/`) and `.env` is deliberately copied as `.env`, never folded into `_seed.json` — the bundler honours R-02 and R-07 by construction.

positives.title: Committed pinned lockfile for CLI dev dependencies
positives.description: `libs/cli/uv.lock` (4031 lines) is committed to the repo and pinned with content hashes per wheel/sdist, giving the CLI's own runtime a reproducible dependency tree.

positives.title: Strict TOML schema rejects unknown sections and keys
positives.description: `_parse_config` allowlists exactly `agent`, `sandbox`, `auth`, `memories`, `frontend` and rejects any other top-level section with a clear migration error — preventing silent migration bugs and reducing the chance of accidental scope expansion through config drift.

positives.title: Credentials are deliberately routed around the seeded-memory payload
positives.description: `bundler.py` copies `.env` to `build_dir/.env` separately (R-06/R-07 compliance), and `_build_seed` reads only `AGENTS.md`, `skills/`, and `user/` — environment secrets never enter `_seed.json` which is committed to the LangSmith Hub repo.

log_files.no_logs_note: No durable log artifacts are produced by `deepagents-cli`; deploy and dev paths emit signal via Python `logging` but the CLI never installs a handler (see finding PRAX-2026-05-25-004), and operator-facing decisions (anonymous-auth confirmation, bundle summary, hub-seed outcome) reach the terminal only via `print()`.

raise_posture.weighted_rationale: Partial. The CLI lands in the *Partial* band: its core guarantee — bundle-from-declared-sources-only, explicit anonymous-deploy confirmation, lockfile-pinned dev dependencies — is genuinely operative and earns credit, but adjacent controls are uneven. Validation is real but porous (TLS-vs-plain-HTTP not enforced on MCP transports, no version pin on remote MCP servers carried into the bundle), there is no logging surface the operator can replay after a deploy, and there is no evidence of adversarial testing or a current threat model for this post-0.1.0 surface. The weighted overall of 2.00 reflects a competent narrow-purpose tool with several concrete supply-chain and observability gaps worth closing.

categories.name: Limit Your Domain
categories.rationale: The CLI's surface is genuinely narrow and code-enforced: `argparse` accepts only `init`, `dev`, and `deploy` (`deepagents_cli/main.py`), bare invocations print the redirect-to-`deepagents-code` notice and exit non-zero, and `_parse_config` rejects unknown TOML sections at load time. The remit excludes outbound email/web calls and the code matches — the CLI only shells out to the `langgraph` CLI and to the LangSmith hub/tracing endpoints under the developer's credentials.

categories.name: Balance Your Knowledge Base
categories.rationale: The bundler reads only the canonical project layout (`AGENTS.md`, `skills/`, `user/`, `mcp.json`, `subagents/`) — `_build_seed` in `bundler.py` enumerates each source explicitly, and `.env` is copied as `.env` rather than folded into `_seed.json` so secrets stay out of the seeded-memory payload. The CLI does not retrieve external content, but it also does no integrity check on the `skills/` contents it carries into the bundle.

categories.name: Implement Zero Trust
categories.rationale: Operative controls earn credit — anonymous-deploy confirmation in `_deploy()` is a real human-in-the-loop gate, scaffold-overwrite is gated behind `--force`, and MCP `stdio` transports are rejected for deployed contexts in `_validate_mcp_for_deploy`. But that same validator accepts plain `http://` URLs (no TLS-only enforcement), and the generated anonymous `auth.py` ships an `identity = "anonymous"` handler that openly disclaims per-user isolation — operators are warned, not prevented.

categories.name: Manage Your Supply Chain
categories.rationale: A committed `uv.lock` (4031 lines) pins the CLI's own dev dependencies, and the deploy bundle infers its dependency set mechanically from the project's declared providers in `_render_pyproject` rather than letting the user hand-edit. But the generated bundle floors `deepagents==0.5.3` with no upper bound, the root-level `.mcp.json` carries two `type=http` MCP servers with neither a version pin nor an integrity check, and the bundler propagates that same pattern when it copies user-declared `mcp.json` into `_mcp.json`.

categories.name: Build an AI Red Team
categories.rationale: A `THREAT_MODEL.md` exists but is explicitly marked as pre-0.1.0 — it describes the interactive REPL, MCP loader, hooks, and sandbox surfaces that have all moved to `deepagents-code`, and the warning at the top says so. No adversarial test suite, no fuzzing of `deepagents.toml`, and no documented red-team exercise for the deploy/dev/init surface that actually ships here.

categories.name: Monitor Continuously
categories.rationale: The deploy and dev paths use Python `logging` (`logger.info`, `logger.warning`) for bundle-summary and hub-seed events, but the CLI never installs a logging handler — `_load_dotenv` in `config.py` notes this explicitly when it falls back to `sys.stderr.write` for user-visible warnings. Anonymous-deploy confirmations, bundle summaries, and hub-seed outcomes are `print()` only, leaving no durable record after the process exits.
